name: Vaccine Shedding
description: Vaccine shedding is a conspiracy theory that claims individuals who have received vaccines, particularly mRNA COVID-19 vaccines, can transmit viral components or the vaccine itself to unvaccinated individuals, causing adverse effects similar to the illness the vaccine is intended to prevent or other unspecified harms. Proponents often suggest that vaccinated individuals are 'shedding' spike proteins or other vaccine components, leading to symptoms such as menstrual irregularities, headaches, fatigue, or even vaccine-related illnesses in unvaccinated people who are in close proximity. This theory gained traction online during the COVID-19 pandemic, fueled by misinformation and distrust in mainstream science and government institutions. Alleged evidence includes anecdotal reports of unvaccinated individuals experiencing health issues after being near vaccinated people, often misinterpreted or lacking scientific validation. Mainstream scientific consensus refutes the notion of vaccine shedding for mRNA vaccines. These vaccines do not contain live viruses and do not replicate within the body. While viral shedding can occur with some live-attenuated vaccines (e.g., oral polio vaccine), it is a well-understood phenomenon with limited and manageable risks. Studies on mRNA COVID-19 vaccines have shown no evidence of shedding. The anecdotal reports cited by proponents are often attributed to coincidental illnesses, nocebo effects (experiencing negative symptoms due to believing the vaccine will cause harm), or other unrelated health conditions. Health organizations like the CDC and WHO have explicitly stated that mRNA vaccines do not cause shedding.
subtitle: Transmission of vaccine components to others?

claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of unvaccinated individuals experiencing symptoms after being near vaccinated people.
claims.criticism: mRNA vaccines do not contain live viruses and do not replicate; therefore, shedding is not biologically plausible. Anecdotal reports are likely coincidental or due to other factors.

claims.claim: Shedding causes adverse health effects in unvaccinated individuals.
claims.evidence: Reports of menstrual irregularities, headaches, and fatigue in unvaccinated people after contact with vaccinated individuals.
claims.criticism: These symptoms are common and can be caused by a variety of factors. There is no scientific evidence linking them to vaccine shedding. Nocebo effect is a likely contributor.

claims.claim: Vaccine shedding can spread COVID-19 or other vaccine-preventable diseases.
claims.evidence: Claims based on misinterpretations of viral shedding with live vaccines.
claims.criticism: mRNA vaccines do not utilize live viruses and therefore cannot cause transmission of the disease. Viral shedding is a risk with some live-attenuated vaccines, but is not applicable to mRNA vaccines.

keyFigures.name: Sherri Tenpenny
keyFigures.role: Prominent anti-vaccine advocate who promotes vaccine shedding theories.

keyFigures.name: Paul Offit
keyFigures.role: Vaccine expert and critic of anti-vaccine misinformation.

timeline.event: Emergence of vaccine shedding theories during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Continued spread of vaccine shedding misinformation on social media.
